title: Use Sunscreen Properly
summary: Apply sunscreen properly before going outside and know when to reapply.
learnerPromise: You will be able to choose a suitable sunscreen and apply it correctly to protect your skin from sun damage.
whyItMatters: Regular sunscreen use helps reduce the risk of sunburn and long-term skin damage, especially during outdoor activities or in sunny climates.
realLifeUses: Applying sunscreen before a walk, hike, or sports practice
Reapplying after swimming or heavy sweating
Choosing the right sunscreen for a beach day or outdoor job
Helping a younger sibling or friend put on sunscreen correctly
materialsNeeded: Sunscreen lotion, spray, or stick with SPF 30 or higher
result: Sunscreen applied evenly to all exposed skin with a reapplication plan in mind.
youWillLearn: Choose a sunscreen with an appropriate SPF and broad-spectrum protection
Apply enough sunscreen to cover all exposed skin evenly
Know when to reapply, such as after swimming or sweating
Check the expiration date and storage instructions
miniChallenge: Apply sunscreen to your face, neck, arms, and hands 15 minutes before going outside. Set a reminder to reapply in two hours.
steps: Check the sunscreen label for SPF, broad-spectrum claim, and expiration date.
Squeeze out a generous amount and dot it on all exposed areas.
Rub it in evenly, making sure to cover ears, back of neck, and tops of feet if exposed.
Wait about 15 minutes before going into direct sun.
Set a timer or reminder to reapply every two hours, or sooner after swimming or heavy sweating.
completionCriteria: You can explain what SPF means in simple terms
You have applied sunscreen to all exposed skin areas
You know when you should reapply and why
commonProblems: Using too little sunscreen, which reduces protection
Missing spots like ears, lips, or the part in your hair
Forgetting to reapply after getting wet or sweaty
tips: Use about a shot-glass amount for your whole body on a beach day
Choose a water-resistant formula if you will be swimming or sweating
Wear a hat and seek shade during midday hours when the sun is strongest
helpBoundary: If you get a severe sunburn with blistering, fever, or extreme pain, contact a medical professional for guidance.
